posts.caption: What would "The Odyssey" be without Penelope, the woman Odysseus goes to hell and back for? Yet, in comparison to the rich depiction in Homer's epic poem, Christopher Nolan’s Penelope (played incredibly by @AnneHathaway) feels flimsy and truncated, leaving viewers wanting more. And this is far from the first time Nolan has received such criticism for women characters; he’s faced similar analysis throughout his career, from Talia al Ghul and Rachel Dawes in his Batman trilogy to Emily Blunt’s turn as Kitty Oppenheimer in "Oppenheimer." At the link in bio, one Bazaar writer gives an honest review of "The Odyssey."

For @lolamansell, documenting tour life is all about trust and timing. 

Equipped with her camera and a keen intuition, Mansell has been on the road documenting @oliviadeano’s “The Art of Loving” world tour, the first of its kind for the Grammy Award-winning artist, in cinematic fashion. Between candid pre-show moments, mid-set outfit changes, and the final confetti fall, Mansell is there, ensuring everything is recorded in intimate detail. 

“I hope through my images, you can see Olivia not only as an incredible performer,” Mansell says, “but as someone who brings connection and joy wherever she goes.”

Click the link in bio to dive deeper into Mansell’s mind. 

✍️: @baileyangelll 
📸: @lolamansell

posts.alt_text:
Introducing our final cover star of the spring issue of HIGHSNOBIETY, @kaytranada. Kay is the type of artist that just gets you off your feet, whose beats take you through the movement. His success has amounted to Grammys, collaborations with artists such as @kaliuchis, and a change to the whole electronic scene. 

What comes as a surprise is that it was all a “happy accident,” as the artist puts it. Kay’s real passion lay in the creation of dark and moody sample-driven hip-hop. Montreal shit, as he so succinctly put it — and the very music he grew up with.  We spoke to Kaytranda about his journey, and how he’s ready to go back to his roots and burrow into the underground with plans to drop “Mixtapes, beat tapes, EPs.” Kaytranada will be here: tinkering, listening, conjuring something unlike anything that came before.   

Read more about Kay and how he’s doing things his own way at the link in bio. 

By @pxthompson
Photographed by @nad_1n3
Styled by @thevonford
Photo Assistant @franziskusd
Hair & Makeup @adiamhabtezion
Video: @andrea___mena, @byeemj, @y.lanyi 
Casting & Talent Manager: @dasia.schow 
Styling Assistant: @the.daily.zazz
Seamstress @_a.r.a.i_
Production Asst: @fuckhughman

posts.caption:
@stoneisland brought its football community together in NYC, and the beers were on the brand.⁠
⁠
Part of its North American Community Pub Tour, the match-day gathering celebrated Stone Island’s longstanding football ties by taking over a local pub, opening a tab, and inviting fans to enjoy the World Cup game together.⁠
⁠
Where were you?
📸 @ellingtonhammond

At the 2026 World Cup, Lamine Yamal was wearing archive accessories throughout championship day. What many people don’t know is it was his last minute effort to show his drip on the field was from a hidden showroom in NYC, @onbondshowroom
“Most stores wouldn’t have the range to carry both that Gucci bag and a niche archive runway Y3 piece. He’s an adidas athlete so finding rare Y3 shades for him was perfect utility for the game.”, says owner Dax Galak.
Originally introduced as part of Yohji Yamamoto’s adidas @adidasy3 collaboration, the 2009 Y-3 runway sunglasses have become a highly sought-after archival collector’s piece, and were the perfect winning piece for Yamal. The @gucci
1955 Horsebit Mini Bag remains one of Gucci’s most recognizable modern silhouettes, and is a piece that is older than the player himself.
For Yamal, it really just is part of his personality. “He has an affinity towards fun accessories.”, Galak says— which shows the growing love for archival and luxury fashion for the sport as a whole.
“Lamine was really nice and came with his whole family, his brother Kayne played around the space which we thought was funny”, says Galak of the player’s time at the showroom.
📷: Getty

Long before tennis-core became the internet’s latest obsession, @filaeurope unequivocally set the standard for the sport’s style. Now, the brand is revisiting that legacy with the Court Series Capsule Collection for Spring/Summer 2027. ⁠
⁠
Paying homage to four iconic tennis cities–Melbourne, Paris, London and New York–the collection is all about blending vintage court references with a dynamic, modern energy.⁠
⁠
Read more about the collection by hitting the link in bio.

Everything looks AI-generated these days, to the point that even genuinely creative, human-made projects get mistaken for machine-made work.⁠
⁠
But creative intention always reveals itself, whether subtle or unmistakable. This recent @gozzilah project for @ospreyeurope explores how intention shapes creative language, and what gets lost when generative tools flatten it into something surface-level.⁠
España ganó la World Cup 🇪🇸🏆 And gave us airport outfits full of designer bags. We couldn’t stop looking at the fashion... ¿Qué bag fue tu favorito?👀

posts.caption:
Not only was @gabbriette the first @matieresfecales bride, but she served three custom looks by the Parisian designer. The pre-wedding look was built from a vintage black gown she already owned, while her actual wedding dress was fully custom. Complete with a triple bustle and a train of torn-edge tulle, it was a masterpiece. Her final look: a sleek lambskin leather suit set, topped with a riding hat by @stephenjonesmillinery. 🖤👰‍♀️
Images: Courtesy of Matières Fécales

posts.caption:
“I like this idea of covert, technical details.” @greglaboratory
Greg Laboratory, by Greg Jackson, is an independent New York–based label that blurs the line between technical wear and everyday tailoring. With meticulous focus on detail and function, each piece contributes to a uniform-style collection that combines material science with innovative design.
Before launching his label, Jackson built an extensive career in sportswear, which continually informs his approach today.
Photo: Hypebeast, Greg Laboratory,
Video: Hypebeast, @bijansosnowski

posts.caption:
Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
Looks like we already have the best dressed bride of 2026, and the title goes go...#Gabbriette! 🖤
In her wedding to The 1975's frontman #MattyHealy, the model chose to sport not one, not two, but THREE custom looks from the Paris based design duo @matieresfecales. This major moment for the up-and-coming the house comes right off the heels of #Zendaya wearing the viral angel wing gown to the NYC premiere of #TheOdyssey and #LadyGaga sporting a custom number from them at the Grammys earlier in the year. We love to see new designers winning, and for more on her looks, go to the link in bio.
📷: Courtesy of @matieresfecalesparis

posts.caption:
Sahher Bambba (@sahherbambba) is exactly where she’s always wanted to be. From landing her breakthrough role as the unapologetic Karishma Talwar in The Ba**ds of Bollywood to navigating the entertainment industry as an outsider, she believes that what’s meant for her will always find its way. There’s an undeniable energy about her, and we got a glimpse of it during our latest cover shoot.
For our latest Youthquake cover, amidst LSA's official food month, Sahher joined us for a midnight adventure at a sparkly convenience store, where she was quite literally like a kid in a candy store. One moment, she’s climbing into a shopping cart; the next, she’s turning up the heat in the frozen section. Her infectious joy is impossible to miss.
Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.
Editor: Analita Seth (@analitaseth)
Shoot produced by Six Ways to Sunday (@sixwaystosundayofficial_)
Photographer: Sahil Khan (@sssahilkhann) (@sixwaystosundayofficial_)
Video: Parth Dave (@theshutterup__) (@sixwaystosundayofficial_)
Stylist: Karnika Kabra (@karnika_kabra)
Produced by: Chirrag Guha (@guha.archie)
Interviewed by: Tanzim Pardiwalla (@pepper_tan)
Makeup: Pooja Fernandes (@makeupwali)
Hair by: Seema (@hairstories_byseema)
Artist Reputation Management: Communiqué Film PR (@communiquefilmpr)
Hospitality Partner: Food Square (@foodsquareindia)
On Sahher: Outfit from @khhouseofkhaddar, Accessories from @mnsh.design, Eyewear from @vogueeyewear @essilorluxottica
[LSA Cover Star, Sahher Bambba, Actress, July 2026]
posts.alt_text:
Gen Z has often been questioned about how serious they really are. This time around they’re proving why they might be the most unseriously serious generation ever.

It’s time we take the Gen Z vocab seriously, because it’s gotten a whole lot of old words redefined that prove what they’re on the streets for.

posts.alt_text:
Sahher Bambba (@sahherbambba) is exactly where she’s always wanted to be. From landing her breakthrough role as the unapologetic Karishma Talwar in The Ba**ds of Bollywood to navigating the entertainment industry as an outsider, she believes that what’s meant for her will always find its way. There’s an undeniable energy about her, and we got a glimpse of it during our latest cover shoot.

For our latest Youthquake cover, amidst LSA's official food month, Sahher joined us for a midnight adventure at a sparkly convenience store, where she was quite literally like a kid in a candy store. One moment, she’s climbing into a shopping cart; the next, she’s turning up the heat in the frozen section. Her infectious joy is impossible to miss.

Read the full cover story at the link in our bio.

Un rovescio a una mano, raro nel tennis di oggi, e un gioco elegante e variato: @Lore_Musetti, 24 anni, ha aperto il 2026 conquistando il quinto posto del ranking ATP, prima che un infortunio alla gamba sinistra lo costringesse a rinunciare al Roland Garros. Global ambassador di #BottegaVeneta, il tennista si racconta a @LOfficielItalia, ritratto dagli scatti di @RaffGrosso e con lo styling di @Fernando.Echeverria_, vestendo un total look della collezione #Fall2026, firmato dalla direttrice creativa @Louise_Trotter_. Tra scaramanzie da spogliatoio, il bronzo olimpico di Parigi, l'ammirazione per il tennis di Alcaraz e la passione per la cucina: «Colpire la pallina, il suono che fa quando la colpisco: è questo che mi rende felice». Leggi l’intervista a cura di @Fabia.DiDrusco al link in bio e sul nuovo numero de @LOfficielItalia, ora in edicola.

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @TravisScott!
The Houston-born artist has spent the past year moving between worlds, crafting the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia’ from his Malibu studio while preparing for his much-anticipated acting debut as a bard in Christopher Nolan’s @theodysseymovie, in theaters July 17.
From music to film to fashion, Scott’s creative universe continues to expand. He also stars alongside Owen Wilson in the upcoming comedy ‘Rolling Loud’ and maintains long-running partnerships with @Nike and @Oakley, all while leading @cactusjack into its next chapter.
Yet at the center of it all remains the music. “I’ve been mad inspired over the last year and a half,” Scott says. “I’ve been buckled down and back building beats from scratch, just creating and pushing the sound.”
Read our full cover story at the link in bio as Scott opens up about the creative forces that drive him, his big-screen debut, and the importance of self-care.

posts.caption:
Un luogo dove storia, cultura e creatività si incontrano. Per la prima volta in assoluto, @Dior presenterà una sfilata in Irlanda, scegliendo l’iconico Trinity College Dublin come scenario per la collezione Fall 2027. Casa della leggendaria Old Library e del Book of Kells, l’università è più di un semplice punto di riferimento: è un simbolo del patrimonio intellettuale e culturale dell’Irlanda.
Per @jonathan.anderson, la location ha un significato ancora più profondo. Come ha raccontato: «Sfilare al Trinity College Dublin è la realizzazione di un sogno. Ho iniziato la mia carriera nella moda a Dublino, quindi tornare qui è qualcosa di profondamente personale. Presentare questa collezione in un contesto così straordinario — in un’istituzione con una storia culturale così importante — è un vero onore».
Un ritorno alle origini, una prima volta storica per Dior e una celebrazione del luogo tanto quanto della moda.
Photo Getty Images and Dior
#Dior #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
Una sposa, reinventata. Per il suo matrimonio con Matty Healy, @Gabbriette è diventata la prima sposa di Matières Fécales, segnando il debutto del marchio d’avanguardia nel bridal. Tre creazioni su misura hanno accompagnato ogni momento della celebrazione: da un abito vintage nero reinterpretato a un sontuoso abito da sposa in taffetà di seta, fino a un completo in morbida pelle per l’after party, tutti accomunati dall’iconica tecnica *déchiré*. Completano i look gli accessori realizzati insieme a Stephen Jones e Christian Louboutin per Matières Fécales, in una visione del matrimonio che riscrive i codici della couture.
Photo: Matières Fécales

His work has brought him to countless destinations across the globe, but @TravisScott still has one major stop left on his bucket list. “I hit every continent on the last tour except Antarctica,” he says. “It’s kind of crazy.”
For Scott, travel is about more than seeing new places—it’s about expanding perspective, and he encourages leaning into exploration whenever possible. “Go touch earth and go actually interact with this world,” he says. “It could change your mindset.”

posts.caption:
The 2026 July/August issue is here!
At the center of this year’s Summer Entertainment Issue is @TravisScott, who opens up about working on the follow-up to 2023’s ‘Utopia,’ the travels and experiences fueling his next chapter, and making his acting debut as part of a star-studded ensemble in Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey.’
As much as we love a blockbuster with a stacked A-list cast, championing fresh talent is always top of mind. Inside the issue, we spotlight eight rising stars whose work this summer demands your attention, from ‘Outer Banks’ star @MadisonBaileyBabe to Avantika of ‘Not Suitable for Work’ to @sashacalle of ‘Sugar.’
It’s also a blockbuster time for literary escapes, as the rise of #BookTok turns reading into an unexpected status symbol that’s shaping summer travel. Looking ahead to the Fall collections, we explore the age-old question of whether sex still sells, given the state of undress on the runway, red carpet, and social feeds in 2026. Stay tuned for the issue hitting newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
When your favorite artist announces that they are going on tour, do you scramble to find an outfit that fits within its visual universe, simply rock their merchandise, or decide not to follow the theme at all? Before the age of the Internet, one would simply buy a ticket, throw on a band tee, and jam out to an artist’s live set.
However, with rising costs that have impacted ticket sales, the experience has turned into an occasion to go all out for. And thanks to social media, the ways that we dress for a concert, performance, or festival have been elevated to the max.
From the friendship bracelets that took over @taylorswift’s #ErasTour to @chappellroan’s distinct themes at each stop during her ‘Rise and Fall of a Midwest Princess’ tour, artists know the impact their music has on their fans, and fashion is the ultimate method to unite. Read more on the rise of the concert uniform at the link in our bio.
Photos Getty Images
#taylorswift #beyoncé #harrystyles #sabrinacarpenter

posts.caption:
“My general rule of thumb is, ‘Have I done it before? And does it feel like something I’d be able to do with my eyes shut?’ In which case, I probably won’t do it,” @himeshjpatel told L’OFFICIEL back in 2024.
It’s a philosophy that’s landed him in Christopher Nolan’s world not once, but twice. After 2020’s ‘Tenet’ — where he says “the scale was undeniable” — Patel now steps into ‘The Odyssey’ as Eurylochus, Odysseus’s trusted second-in-command. As ‘The Odyssey’ shapes up to be one of the year’s biggest films, head to the link in our bio to read more from Patel on staying flexible as an actor, his drive to keep evolving, and who’s on his dream collaborator list.
Photography @zebdaemen
Styled by @wayperry_edit

posts.caption:
Pop music has always been obsessed with the future. Every era promised the next big sound, the next superstar, the next cultural reset. But somewhere over the last few years, the industry’s gaze quietly shifted backward. Today’s biggest musical moments increasingly aren’t about discovering something new—they’re about rediscovering something familiar.
A surprise boyband reunion sends social media into a frenzy. A decade-old song suddenly climbs the charts after going viral on TikTok. Stadium tours celebrating albums released twenty years ago sell out within minutes. Long-dormant artists tease returns that generate headlines before a single note of new music has even been heard. Increasingly, pop isn’t just selling songs. It’s selling memories.
Nostalgia has become far more than a passing trend. It’s become infrastructure. The music industry has learned how to monetize nostalgia—and we’re more than willing to buy back our childhoods. Read more at the link in our bio.
Photos Getty Images
#mileycryus #justinbieber #hilaryduff

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Lionel Messi’s passion for cars is every bit as extraordinary as his career on the pitch.
Valued at more than $55 million, the football icon’s collection ranges from ultra-rare classics to modern hypercars and luxury SUVs. Highlights include the legendary Ferrari 335 S Spider Scaglietti, a Pagani Zonda Tricolore, Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG, Ferrari F430 Spider, Maserati GranTurismo MC Stradale, multiple Audi models, Range Rovers, and a Cadillac Escalade.
Blending historic collector cars with high-performance supercars and everyday luxury vehicles, Messi’s garage reflects a deep appreciation for automotive craftsmanship—making it one of the most remarkable collections owned by any athlete.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Mercedes-Maybach has unveiled the new 2027 GLS 680, elevating its flagship luxury SUV with more power, greater refinement, and an even stronger focus on bespoke craftsmanship.
The updated model is powered by a 603-hp twin-turbocharged 4.0-litre V8 paired with a 48-volt mild-hybrid system, delivering smoother performance while enhancing the effortless driving experience Maybach is known for. The exterior receives an illuminated Maybach grille, redesigned lighting, new 23-inch forged wheels, and expanded MANUFAKTUR personalization options, while the cabin introduces fresh interior themes, rose gold accents, Dolby Atmos audio, upgraded rear executive seating, and an even quieter, more luxurious environment.
More than a facelift, the new GLS 680 reinforces Mercedes-Maybach’s position at the pinnacle of the ultra-luxury SUV segment, where handcrafted comfort, cutting-edge technology, and effortless performance remain the ultimate expression of modern luxury.
@MercedesBenzUSA #MercedesBenz #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Hamilton and Christopher Nolan have unveiled a timepiece inspired by one of cinema’s greatest epics.
Created to celebrate Nolan’s latest film, The Odyssey, the Khaki Field Auto The Odyssey Limited Edition translates the mythology of Odysseus into a collectible watch. Crafted in bronze and limited to 2,112 pieces, it features a dial inspired by the hero’s helmet, sword-shaped hands, an engraved titanium caseback bearing Christopher Nolan’s signature, and an exclusive presentation box complete with a replica of Athena’s pin from the film.
More than a movie tie-in, the release continues Hamilton’s long-standing collaboration with Christopher Nolan, blending Swiss watchmaking with cinematic storytelling in a limited-edition piece designed for both collectors and film enthusiasts.
@HamiltonWatch #HamiltonWatch #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Lacoste is giving its most iconic symbol a starring role.
For Summer 2026, the French fashion house has placed its legendary crocodile at the heart of a playful new campaign, transforming the emblem from a subtle logo into the collection’s defining character. Set against a sun-soaked Riviera backdrop, the imagery features relaxed tailoring, classic polos, pastel tones, vintage convertibles, and oversized inflatable crocodiles blending French elegance with effortless summer style.
Rather than reinventing its identity, Lacoste celebrates the codes that made it iconic, proving that one of fashion’s most recognizable logos can still feel fresh, modern, and unmistakably luxurious
@rafael_moura @lacoste

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
Two years into marriage, nine into love. For Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero), togetherness has never been about grand gestures. It’s love distilled to its essentials—a shared home, an easy silence, and the same laughter that started it all.
For Manifest’s July-August cover, the couple lets us in on what “forever” actually looks like, unguarded and unhurried. “I think people really tend to scare you about how your life is going to change after marriage,” says Sonakshi, adding, “but the beauty of it, for us at least, is that things haven’t changed at all. It still feels like I’m his girlfriend and he’s my boyfriend.”
Between banter and blessing, stillness and spontaneity, they’ve found the rare thing most couples spend a lifetime chasing. A forever kind of love.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Chairperson: Avarna Jain (@avarna_jain)

posts.alt_text:
#ManifestCover

Marriage didn’t change Sonakshi Sinha (@aslisona) and Zaheer Iqbal (@iamzahero). It simply gave their love a new address.

“We dated for seven years and knew everything about each other before marriage,” says Zaheer.

“That’s why we got married,” Sonakshi adds. “We wanted to learn to co-exist, to live in harmony and happiness.”
Perhaps that’s what makes their story feel so effortless. Not the absence of disagreements, but the certainty that they’ll always find their way back to each other.

Our July-August issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
During last month’s men’s runway shows in Paris, paper-bag pants made many appearances. Excess fabric sits above the belt — here, a crucial accessory — creating a shape like a fabric pouch that’s been cinched.
For years, the style has been largely aimed at women. But at the last Saint Laurent men’s show, the pants were plentiful. Our fashion reporter, Jacob Gallagher, also encountered them at Celine and Lanvin. Willy Chavarria stacked two sets of belt loops on top of each other, creating a dealer’s choice of paper-bag trousers.
“That paper-bag trousers migrated onto the men’s runways landed, to me, like yet more evidence that pants are the defining items of men’s fashion over this decade,” @jacobwgallagher wrote in his latest newsletter.
At the link in our bio, read more about how men’s fashion is experimenting with the paper-bag waist. And subscribe to The Fashions newsletter for more on what’s happening in fashion now, straight to your inbox. Photo by Kristy Sparow/Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
A reader asked our fashion critic: "Why is it so difficult to find fashionable clothes that have sleeves? I know it’s hot out, but the skin on my upper limbs is no longer something I want to show off, and increasingly I feel there is no alternative. What is someone to do if they want to survive the current temperatures and look stylish but aren’t happy about revealing their arms?"

What’s behind the rise of sleeveless clothing? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman explains the trend and offers advice on how to manage it. Photo by Getty Images

posts.caption:
These days it seems every major sporting event is an opportunity for a red carpet, so it would not have been surprising if the World Cup final on Sunday were the same. But the first World Cup halftime show, potentially the most style-conscious moment of the match, turned out to be its least fashionable moment. In part because almost every participant seemed to have the same idea when it came to clothes: It’s the jersey, baby.
Madonna set the tone with a pair of mauve silk Saint Laurent shorts along with a matching personalized warm-up jacket — with a corset and lace thigh-highs. Gustavo Dudamel, conducting members of the Simón Bolívar Symphony Orchestra of Venezuela and the New York Philharmonic, wore a jacket customized on the back with his name and the number “1” inspired by a soccer jersey. The members of BTS wore tracksuits, leathers and soccer-like jerseys. Even Miss Piggy wore a bedazzled pink jersey-adjacent top.
Jerseys may be the most collectible, customizable, recognizable symbol of the beautiful game, fandom and the World Cup, but they derive their power from the teams and people they represent. Their presence at halftime "turned them into kitsch, rather than relevant costume," our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman wrote. At the link in our bio, read more from @vvfriedman's analysis of the World Cup halftime style. Photos by Julio Cortez/@apnews, Eugene Gologursky/Getty Images for Global Citizen, @hiroko.masuike, Carl Recine/Getty Images

posts.caption:
No maternity clothes? No problem. Gone (mostly) are the days of hiding a baby bump under boxy maternity wear. Instead, expectant mothers are going the Rihanna route: reveal, style and exalt a new, if temporary, silhouette.
There was once a time when showing a pregnant belly was considered scandalous. Coming of age with more body-positive messaging, younger generations have a different attitude toward the pregnant body.
Seeking continuity in their style as their bodies change, many pregnant women are making do with whatever they already have in their closets, or are pulling from their partner's wardrobe. And for those who are fashion-minded, figuring out how to put outfits together out of non-maternity wear — and deciding how to frame, or flaunt, a bump — can be a fun exercise. Swipe through to see how pregnant women are styling their baby bumps, and tap the link in our bio to see more photos. Photos by @dollyfaibyshev

posts.caption:
Hats with words stitched across the crown in an inverted arch are ubiquitous in a certain branch of men's fashion. You can buy hats with “Nashville” or “Drunk” or “EBITDA”  or even "Jesus" written upside down.
The capsized cap has particular purchase among pro athletes and those who track them. Steph Curry has worn a cap with “Bay Area” inverted. The golfer Jose Luis Ballester wore an upside down “Sun Devils” hat as he shot his way through the Masters last year. The Cowboys quarterback Dak Prescott is one of many Texas pros to put on the upside-down Dallas hat.
The hats are "more merch than fashion," our style reporter @jacobwgallagher writes in his newsletter. "Their purpose isn’t to look interesting but to shout an interest," he continues. The concept has spread far and wide, but where did the trend start? Read more at the link in our bio and subscribe to The Fashions newsletter. Photos by Noah Graham/NBAE via Getty Images, USA TODAY Sports via Reuters, Andrew Redington/Getty Images

posts.caption:
When most women think of Chanel ballet flats, they think of them in their prim and proper Rue Cambon element. The classic tan-and-black pair have a reputation as polished and Parisian or manicured and uptown.
But recently, a subset of creative, upwardly mobile women are traipsing around in the flats in flashy colors and funky materials. Often in physical jobs and on set for production or styling, these women wear the shoes with baggy cargos, blooming skirts or low-slung jeans, rather than the tweed skirt suits they may have been previously imagined with.
“They’re like a utility shoe,” Ariella Starkman, a creative strategist and producer in New York, said. “They add a level of polish when I’m doing the dirty work,” she added.
Unlike luxury bags, shoes often do not appreciate in value. But Chanel flats appear to be a different story. Tap the link in our bio to read more about how Chanel flats have become the shoe du jour for fashion girls. Photos by Getty Images

posts.caption:
#Gabbriette’s gothic @matieresfecales wedding wardrobe was one of the most beautiful I’ve seen this year and a welcome change from the minimalist gowns that have dominated bridal trends.
The ceremony gown, titled “Gabriella,” was a mutated and distressed Victorian-inspired silk taffeta dress reminiscent of Winona Ryder’s red dress in Bram Stoker’s #Dracula (1992), complete with a four layer bustle.
For the rehearsal dinner, an old Maison Margiela dress of the brides was modified by adding layers of hand-cut, distressed tulle. The third look was a tailored leather suit paired with a satin top hat by #StephenJones. Each look completed with a custom pair of #ChristianLouboutin x #MatièresFécales curved heels.
posts.alt_text:
The #MiddleEast has produced some of the world’s greatest designers, but why do so many come from #Lebanon? Sat at the crossroads of Silk Road trade routes linking Asia, Europe, and Africa, Lebanon developed a tradition of textiles and embroidery, with skills passed down through generations.

Couture is a huge market In the Middle East due to the social culture of weddings and events. While Haute Couture is an institutional system in Paris,  the concept has existed for centuries across India, Pakistan, the Middle East, and China. In the West, couture works as a prestige marketing tool, elsewhere this level of craftsmanship has always been part of how people dress.

Unlike heritage brands which became corporatised and part of conglomerates, Lebanon’s couture boom produced independent designers like #ZuhairMurad, #ElieSaab, and #GeorgesHobeika, who still lead their own houses. Their success built on craftsmanship, personal reputation, and loyal private clients.

posts.caption:
At first glance, Look 38 from #PierpaoloPiccioli’s #Balenciaga couture debut may look simple, but it’s an incredible feat of tailoring, textile engineering, and couture pattern cutting.
Instead of separate layers like inner corset, a structural foundation and lining, the engineering is absorbed into one layer of the dress itself. Multiple layers of silk gazar are stacked, topstitched, and shaped to the exact measurements of the wearer, making a gown that is sculptural yet remarkably lightweight.
Rather than relying on rigid corsetry to sculpt the body, the silhouette is created entirely through meticulous tailoring and an intricate internal structure, the type of innovation one expects from modern-day #HauteCouture.
Shoutout to the Balenciaga atelier who worked on this look @mathieugoosse @e.quentin @rvsimond

posts.caption:
Inside the former chapel of a stately Parisian home lies #RogerVivier’s extraordinary archive. The climate-controlled “laboratory” houses more than 50 years of work by one of the world’s most influential shoe designers.
@rogervivier transformed footwear, pioneering the stiletto heel and the curved “Choc” heel. His work for  #ChristianDior gave shoes the same level of artistry and craftsmanship as Haute Couture. Decades later, his influence continues to shape contemporary fashion, with echoes of his designs still visible at houses including #Dior, #Prada, and #SaintLaurent

posts.caption:
#VictoriaBeckham truly is the defining #WAG. She created such a distinct aesthetic that it is still referenced today. Her husband may have been captain but she famously never wore an England #football shirt. Instead opting for slogan tank tops paired with tiny shorts or low-rise jeans, oversized sunglasses, Louboutins and a Birkin.
She knew exactly who she was and rather than dressing like a typical football fan, she dressed like Victoria Beckham: international pop star and an emerging fashion figure. 20 years later and while her style is much more refined, we will never forget the OG wag.
posts.alt_text:
#Gabbriette’s gothic @matieresfecales wedding wardrobe was one of the most beautiful I’ve seen this year and a welcome change from the minimalist gowns that have dominated bridal trends.

The ceremony gown, titled “Gabriella,” was a mutated and distressed Victorian-inspired silk taffeta dress reminiscent of Winona Ryder’s red dress in Bram Stoker’s #Dracula (1992), complete with a four layer bustle.

posts.caption:
Kim Jones’ last collection looked very feminine to me. The silhouettes, the choice of fabrics, the draping, and the fluid, silky cuts all leaned toward a traditionally feminine aesthetic.
Fashion has always evolved, and there is nothing wrong with that. Throughout history, styles have moved between men’s and women’s wardrobes. Nowadays, most people don’t object to women wearing clothing that was once considered exclusively men’s—such as trousers, shirts, or tailoring—as long as it still reflects their own style and identity.
Personally, I believe there should still be a balance between creativity and identity. Fashion can push boundaries, but it should also remain respectable and reasonable. I appreciate designs that allow room for innovation while preserving a recognizably masculine silhouette for men and a feminine silhouette for women.
Of course, this is only my personal preference. Different cultures have different traditions and expectations, and I respect that not everyone shares the same view. Fashion should leave room for individual expression while also respecting cultural diversity and personal values.

posts.caption:
Justin Bieber for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
This is a real piece of history for us all. The past 10 years of lessons and learning have led up to this moment and I couldn’t be happier to share it with you all.
Some of you may be new followers who discovered us last week and some of you may have been here since day 1, either way this is for everyone who aligns with what it means to be an outlander.
Also, this is a great time to introduce myself for the first time in 10 years. My name is @callum.mccaff & we are all Outlander. I was born in 1996 so we priced the magazine at £19.96.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com
PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
🫂 @lilbieber
📸 @sweetcian
🔗 @freenjoy
posts.alt_text:
Naomi Osaka for Outlander Magazine wearing her custom Nike Roland Garros looks designed by Nike & Kevin Germanier!

Roland Garros’ best dressed just raised the bar again. Naomi’s custom Nike looks were designed by Kevin Germanier, the couture designer turning upcycled materials into something iconic. Clay court season needed a moment like this, so we took her to an authentic orange clay court in paris to match the energy she’ll be surrounded by this week😤🤝

posts.caption:
Travis Scott for Issue 01: “The First Ever” Outlander Magazine In-Print!
GANG, TRAVIS SCOTT IN HERMÈS FOR OUR FIRST EVER MAGAZINE COVER. THIS IS UNHEARD OF, AND BETTER YET? IT WAS FOR OUTLANDER MAGAZINE. I AM HUMBLED.
WOW, what a moment. My only wish in this life is that what I’ve spent the last 10 years building acts as a strong form of inspiration for your own creative careers - I came from no fashion background, I had no connections & I had no idea what I was doing, but, I did it anyway.
If you didn’t know by now, my name is @callum.mccaff , I founded Outlander - and I’m here to remind everyone that PRINT ISN’T DEAD.
Available worldwide at www.outlandermag.com

posts.caption:
India's Renee Noronha has won first place in the 18-24 category at the Ironman Philippines. Completing the gruelling challenge at 15:55:37, the 20-year-old IIT Madras student upheld held her record of being the youngest Indian female Ironman finisher.
Renee battled torrential rains and unforgiving temperatures to complete the race. In conversation with SheThePeople, the triathlete also revealed that she participated on day 3 of her period, making the victory a profound reminder of her strength and resilience as a woman.
With this win, Renee has now qualified for the Ironman World Championship Hawaii, scheduled for October 2026. She is also on a mission to set a new Guinness world record to become the youngest person in the world to do a full Ironman on 6 continents. She is on track to achieve this by April 2027, at the age of 21.
Read full interview at www.shethepeople.tv. | @renee.noronha
#Ironman #Philippines #sports full Ironman challenge, women in sports, women in endurance sports, mumbai sports, Chennai sports, IIT Madras student, women in STEM, ironman Kona, triathlon, marathon, menstrual awareness

posts.caption:
As Christopher Nolan's The Odyssey makes headlines worldwide, along with Matt Damon's ripped look, the Oscar winner shares an unexpected behind-the-scenes revelation about the film. He captured everyone’s attention when he mentioned that behind some of the film's memorable arm shots, his stunt double was a woman, Devyn Dalton.
Damon extended his appreciation, saying he was impressed by her biceps and incredible strength. “When we did the forced perspective stuff of the Laestrygonians - I don’t know if there’s been a thing about how we did it- there were these stunt men who were all seven feet,” the actor shared, reflecting on The Odyssey. “And then they got stuntmen who were under five feet, and so my double was a woman, a female stunt performer, who had the greatest arms I’ve ever seen,” he added.
#theodyssey Matt Damon, Zendaya, Devyani Dalton, entertainment industry, stunt double, action scenes, cinema, viral, trending

posts.caption:
‘Do as I say, or else I’ll make that video viral’: the one sentence could make a woman’s chest heave with anguish, rushing all her blood to the mind. The perverts keep looking for a new tactic to harass or assault women. Earlier, they used hidden cameras to record private moments; now the entrance of artificial intelligence has substituted other devices. AI has provided them with a facility to generate videos that exist on your screen alone. With every passing day, a woman’s fear of online abuse strengthens. The story of their torment continues.
Indian author Meena Kandasamy portrays this nervousness of women in her latest book ‘Fieldwork as a Sex Object’, where the protagonist becomes a prey to a deepfake video. She hesitates to reach out to her parents, predicting their reactions. As the story progresses, she feels trapped between truth and fabrication. The novel exposes the devastating human cost of digital misogyny in an age of surveillance, outrage and technological manipulation.
In a conversation with SheThePeople, Meena Kandasamy talks about identity theft, the dilemma of writers in the digital age, and gives a message to young girls nursing the ambition of becoming writers.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full interview by @_shivanilamba
Fiction writing, poetry, Meena Kandasamy, Indian authors, AI, deepfake, misogyny, feminism, social media, novel, fieldwork as a sex object, identity theft, digital age, technology, AI generated videos, sexual assault, online abuse, viral, trending, explore

Indian badminton player PV Sindhu enters the Japan Open semifinals after her Japanese opponent Nozomi Okuhara’s withdrawal on Friday. After the Malaysian Super 1000 and Australian Super 500, this marks Sindhu's third semifinal of the season.
Chasing her first title of the season, Sindhu will face  Chen Yufei, Tokyo Olympic champion and world No. 4 Chinese shuttler, whom she trails 6-8 in the head-to-head record.

posts.caption:
A growing number of ladies are making the first move, changing the rules of dating. A few months ago, I ran into a newly divorced friend sulking in the corner of a party. Hugs and tears followed until she asked me the all-important question: how do I start again?
My friend had a rather genuine problem; she was married to a national celebrity, and her divorce was rather public too. Since her ex was a famous figure, she discovered she had no suitors thanks to being in the public eye herself. “It’s been a long and lonely year. And no one has even shown interest,” she admitted.
“Don’t wait to be asked out, you make the first move,” I replied. She was aghast; she made a face as if I had asked her to get her tongue pierced. She said she had never asked a man out. “Do it once, cross that bridge, and you’ll find how easy and empowering it is,” I smiled.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full opinion piece by @namratazakaria
#dating #love dating advice, relationships, dating apps, couple reels, first move, bollywood, Deepika Padukone, Queen, A suitable boy, empowerment, feminism

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.
posts.alt_text:
Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.

John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.

If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

Save this. Share it with someone who needs it.

#dior #jonathananderson #stylistelixir

As a fashion stylist, I used to dread this time of year. In August the fashion industry shuts down. Everyone in Europe goes on holiday, which means we get a lot less work. Unless you have planned carefully through the year and have a money reserve, it will hit you hard.
So there are two things to take from this.
First, treat August like a reset month. Look at your portfolio and your Instagram, do they show who you are as a stylist now? Look at your systems, were you paid on time, did you send a contract for every job? Are you happy with all your clients, did they fill you with joy or dread? Write down the gaps, and spend some of August closing the ones you can do yourself.
And secondly, it is okay to take the break. It is okay to have some time off as a freelancer. Your job will not magically disappear because you did not work for a week.
On the 1st of August I am running my Fashion Mentorship Program. It is 6 weeks to reset your career, and you can do it alongside your summer holiday (everything is recorded, so if you miss a week you can catch up). £200 off at checkout. Link in bio 💚
posts.alt_text:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.

It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.

And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.

Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.

Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.

That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. So why do so many talented stylists still treat it like a hobby?
Styling is a craft and a business. The ones who build lasting careers are not the most passionate, they are the ones who assist with intention, pitch well, price properly and deliver reliably. Know your stylist type and diversify with intention, not desperation, because multiple streams mean stability. Let your portfolio show your role and range, not a moodboard dump. Treat pitching as the job, clear emails and follow-ups are professional, not desperate. And build a plan, boundaries, systems and rate confidence, because great styling is half logistics, half delivery.
Reliability is what gets you rehired.
The full build, the templates and the room that holds you to it are inside the Studio, with 100+ working stylists. Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you in free for a week 💚 The price more than doubles in August, so joining now locks it.

posts.caption:
It’s 3am and you’re doing the mental maths again. When is the next job. Will December be quiet. How long will this last.
That 3am maths is a systems problem, not a talent problem. The instability is real, but it is not your fault and it is not your fate. Stability gets built on purpose. A year-round pipeline of warm contacts means you never start from zero. A buffer of a few months of expenses lets you say no to draining clients. Diversifying within styling means one quiet area never takes everything down.
The best time to build all of that is now, calmly, before a slow season forces it.
That is exactly what we set up in the Studio, together, with 100+ working stylists. The pipeline, the buffer, the systems that stop the quiet weeks feeling like the end.
Comment STUDIO and I’ll send you in free for a week 💚

posts.caption:
Algorithms learn from what holds attention, then return increasingly precise versions of it.
A passing interest can begin occupying more of the feed, while other possibilities quietly recede. Repetition gives the fantasy familiarity; personalisation makes it feel self-selected.
A 2025 review of research on echo chambers and ‘information cocoons’ found that algorithmic curation can reinforce existing inclinations by recommending content based on previous interactions, preferences, and behaviour.
Researcher Jeena Joseph describes the identity that emerges from this feedback loop as the “algorithmic self”: a version of the self increasingly interpreted through recommendations, predictive models, and behavioural data.
The result is a blur between discovering a preference and having one repeatedly reflected back.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
posts.alt_text: When opportunity is scarce and independence is expensive, one exam begins to carry the weight of an entire future.

posts.caption:
Founded in 1737, Favre Leuba (@favreleuba) brought 289 years of Swiss watchmaking to Gaurav Gupta’s Light Song presentation in Mumbai. The collaboration signals a move towards authorship, carrying particular weight for a maison whose relationship with India began in 1865.
Relaunched globally in 2024 and making its Watches and Wonders Geneva debut in 2026, Favre Leuba is placing its heritage within new cultural contexts. Light Song, a 70-look collection developed over nine months by more than 200 artisans across 6,000 hours, offered a meeting point between horology and couture: two disciplines in which precision determines the final form. Models and Gupta wore Favre Leuba timepieces on the runway, making the watches part of the collection’s sculptural rhythm.
However, its most compelling outcome arrives later this year, when Gupta’s design language is translated into a Favre Leuba watch.
#FavreLeuba #GauravGupta #Collab

posts.caption:
GLP‑1 drugs like Ozempic and Mounjaro have turned thinness into something you can buy, display, and scroll past. Vanity got rebranded as discipline, productivity, and health.
Pilates classes, protein smoothies, and prescriptions now read as shorthand for success, while the privilege and medical scaffolding behind them vanish from the frame. Social media makes extreme bodies look ordinary. #SkinnyTok pulled in 500 million views before its ban, and Instagram’s #skinny has climbed past 5.7 million posts.
Thinness is treated as responsibility, marketed as proof of self‑control, and flattened into virtue. Discipline gets celebrated, medicine gets hidden, and the result is a culture where thinness equals worth.
GLP‑1 drugs collapsed the gap between wellness and weight loss, making rapid change look effortless. Skinny culture didn’t disappear. It changed costume, and this time it’s sold as self‑improvement.
Full story at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#GLP1drugs #skinnyculture #wellnessculture

posts.caption:
Telugu cinema didn’t rise because Bollywood collapsed. It rose because it built an industry around theatrical ambition, bigger risks, and audiences still willing to show up.
Baahubali expanded what a regional film could become and took that ambition global. Kalki 2898 AD confirmed that spectacle had become the new centre of gravity. In Telugu cinema, risk stopped being the exception and became part of the system.
Bollywood took a different route. Multiplexes narrowed its idea of the audience, corporate studios changed how films were financed, and streaming offered new ways to recover costs. The industry gained safety nets, but lost some of its dependence on the box office.
Bollywood once set the rules. Now its stars are signing Telugu productions and its producers are studying Hyderabad’s scale. The shift is less about one industry having found a perfect formula than about one still being structured to bet on theatres.
For now, the blockbuster machine with the stronger appetite for risk sits in the south.
Full story at the link in bio
By DevRupa Rakshit
#TeluguCinema #Bollywood #PanIndia #BlockbusterCulture #FilmEconomics

posts.alt_text:
London is looking buuuusy!!! The provisional Spring/Summer 2027 schedule is out, with 100+ happenings set to take over the city from September 17–21—and as always, it’s a mix of legacy names and next-gen disruption.

On one side: the anchors. Burberry closing the week under Daniel Lee, Alexander McQueen with Seán McGirr’s LFW co-ed debut, and Mulberry entering a new chapter with Christopher Kane at the helm. On the other: the reason London still matters. Fashion East, BFC NEWGEN, and a wave of emerging designers pushing things forward while the bigger houses hold the line.

If you were thinking LFW is dead, think again. Some of the most influential designers make their debuts here. Keep your eyes out.

#LondonFashionWeek #LFW #SS27

posts.caption:
Did you know Christian Louboutin has a private tower? Because of course he does. Tucked away in Melides, Portugal, “La Folie” is a concrete cylinder designed by architect Tarek Shamma—with quite literally no purpose aside from hosting friends and meditating between late night parties and chats in the conversation pit. What more could you possibly need?
Completed in 2021 after seven years of work, the tower sits on Louboutin’s coastal estate like a physical extension of his world, and he’s not alone. From Yves Saint Laurent’s saturated Moroccan home (now private hotel) to Givenchy’s restrained French château, designers have long treated their homes as permanent expressions of their aesthetic.
At this point, I wouldn’t be surprised if private architectural projects quietly become the next status symbol. Sorry, Birkins bags…
Images: Clément Vayssières
#ChristianLouboutin #Architecture #Portugal

Jonathan Anderson is bringing Dior to Dublin.
on 4 December, the house will present its Fall 2027 collection at Trinity College Dublin’s Old Library, marking Dior’s first-ever show in Ireland.
for Anderson, it’s more than a destination. although he’s from Northern Ireland, he began his career in fashion in Dublin, making the return a full-circle moment, making this his most meaningful Dior show yet.
and if you’ve been paying attention, the clues have been there all along. from the four-leaf clover that announced his Dior appointment to the clover motifs woven into his early collections, Anderson has been quietly celebrating his Irish roots from day one. ☘️

posts.caption:
Even if you know nothing about Masaba Gupta (@masabagupta), at the very least you know that she’s famous. Some people know her simply as Masaba, founder of the fashion label House of Masaba (@houseofmasaba) that, for the last 17 years, has been making playfully printed contemporary Indian clothing. Others know her as the star of the Netflix show ‘Masaba Masaba’. To the chronically online, she’s the powerful influencer, the person brands go to when they want a cool, articulate personality to represent them. The makeup-obsessed know her via LoveChild by Masaba (@lovechildbymasaba), the beauty brand she started in 2021, which has taken her obsession with makeup and turned it into products built specifically around Indian skin tones.
Yet, as she continues to do it all, there is a change underway. As we walk out of her office, Masaba casually drops a piece of news. “I’m moving away from ready-to-wear entirely. I think spring/summer 2026 is the last one we’ll do. The focus is going to be on occasion and bridalwear.” It’s a pretty big declaration, and a timely one, considering that later this month Masaba will be a part of the FDCI Hyundai India Couture Week in New Delhi. Right now, it feels like Masaba is moving into a new era, one where she’s more of a founder and businesswoman than a niche fashion designer.
At the link in bio, @butoolbegum spends the day in the company of the enterprising designer, business owner, and new mom.

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Your vacation may have come to an end, but your skin doesn’t need to show it. We all know that when you get home from a weeks-long getaway—days spent under the hot sun or in the snow, filled with carbs, sugar, and limitless vino (ah, the joy!)—it’s going to reflect on the surface. And even if you’ve spent most of your time getting your step count up, the simple change in your daily diet, temperature, and time zone can cumulatively wreak havoc on your skin as soon as you’re back to home base.
At the link in bio, @anjansachar's nifty guide with four simple routine swaps for best way to reset your skin.
Image courtesy: @annehathaway

posts.caption:
Art, as one troubled artist once said, is the lie that allows us to see the truth. Days after protesting students took over Jantar Mantar in the national capital and streets across India to rally behind education reformer Sonam Wangchuck, art has emerged as the most powerful language to channel creativity into Gen Z’s agitation against exam paper leaks.
Of course, art and politics have always existed in conversation with one another, each shaping and responding to the other. The past week has just reiterated this relationship. A quick scroll through Instagram is enough to see it. Songs from protest rockers and Bollywood OSTs have become the soundtrack to protest Reels, iconic movie scenes have been recontextualised as symbols of solidarity, and artists across disciplines have responded to the moment in real time.
Visual artists are no exception. Some have created new works, while others have reshared older ones that have taken on renewed relevance in light of the ongoing protests. Below, a carousel of thought-provoking artworks, both old and new, that may be varied in style and medium but are equally powerful as symbols of resistance.
Slide 1: Artwork by Sanitary Panels (@sanitarypanels)
Slide 2: Artwork by Santanu Hazarika (@santanu_hazarika_art)
Slide 3: 'Mob' by Sameer Kulavoor (@sam_kulavoor)
Slide 4: Artwork by Sajid Wajid Shaikh (@sajidwajidshaikh)
Slide 5: 'Crushing Voices' by Vikrant Bhise (@vikrantbhise_artist)
Slide 6: Artwork by Jayati Bose (@bosejayati)
Slide 7: Artwork by Siddhesh Gautam (@bakeryprasad)

posts.caption:
"Even before I’d tasted the controversial fruit, I’d photographed the famous “No Durian” sign on the MRT, assuming it was a joke. I hadn’t yet eaten the fresh fruit of legend: the pale yellow custardy lobes prised from a spiky shell. Yet, somehow, like many people who haven’t eaten it yet, I already had a story about it in my head.
I expected to dislike it.
What surprised me wasn’t that my adventurous palate ended up liking durian. It was how quickly curiosity became craving. The fruit I’d wrinkled my nose at on first sniff became, with consecutive visits, a flavour I kept seeking out. Every variety seemed to have its own personality. Every dessert I met sent me looking for the next one. Here, familiarity hadn’t bred contempt but appetite."
At the link in bio, @roshnibajajsanghvi's take on the polarising fruit and where to try it if you're in Singapore this summer.

posts.caption:
At a time when every entertainment-themed conversation revolves around Christopher Nolan’s vision, behold, a show that has just sprung on us like a gift from the gods, but, thankfully, not the Greek or geeky ones. Ride or Die, released on Prime Video (@primevideo) without much fanfare, is all the things a big-ticket buddy action-comedy TV series should be, with one IMAX-sized plus: the buddies happen to be two middle-aged women.
In Ride of Die, we’re introduced to Hannah Waddingham as a WOACA (Woman of a Certain Age)—50 or thereabouts—and being offered a Patek Philippe parting gift from her employers: she’s just not following the rules anymore, and that’s a problem when you’re an undercover assassin. But she’s not going down without a fight. Her “best friend”, Debbie Claybourne, played by the self-assured Octavia Spencer (@octaviaspencer), has spent decades of her life sacrificing her law degree for her husband’s ambitions.
At the link in bio, read @rehana__munir’s full review of the series about two women pushed to the brink.

posts.caption:
How we remember Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey released today, will depend, in part, on the stories we were told before we entered a cinema hall. Or how Homer first reached us in childhood retellings by mothers and teachers. Through echoes maybe of the Mahabharata, that also narrated impossible journeys and flawed destinies. Odysseus, like Arjuna, Karna or Ravana, is among epic characters who live in our imagination long after we have forgotten their battles.
Yet, epics are never just about heroes.
Beyond the production scale, the IMAX filming, the six countries that stand for the ancient Mediterranean, and the thousands as cast and crew who have rebuilt Homer’s 2,800-year-old poem for the screen, lies another story. A delight for those interested in material culture: the recovery of a Bronze Age remembered by Homer and reimagined by Nolan.
When bronze was the defining material. When wood was the framework of civilisation and cloth, Penelope’s political instrument of delay.
As Ithaca waited for Odysseus, Penelope promised to choose a suitor after she had finished weaving a shroud for her father-in-law, Laertes. She wove by day. By night she unravelled every thread. Her loom was not just a domestic object. It was a Queen’s resistance to push back a dreaded future.
Costume designer Ellen Mirojnick has spoken about giving linen, leather, armour and sandals the texture of lived history. Her Mycenaean costumes for kings and soldiers, the drapes of Athena and Calypso, cinematographer Hoyte van Hoytema’s Mediterranean light, together with the craftsmanship of the Trojan horse have tantalised us before the film released.
Bewitchingly, in this era of chrome finishes, AI-generated images and digital smoothness, why do we long for grain, weight, patina, fibres and imperfections? Why does worn linen feel reassuring? Why does wood still move us?
Maybe because materials remember what human minds forget. Before they become cinematic costumes, they bear the marks of the hands that made them. Before they tell stories, they have lived through personal and political history. To find them is a homecoming.
Read the rest via link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text:
This was a bit of a revelation about Darcy’s costumes once I’d started filming. What do you think? Am I being too woo-woo? 😂

#prideandprejudice2005

posts.caption: As much as is really loved the movie and applaud the level of production it had, to me, the costume design was one of its weakest parts. In many cases it was too Greek cliché and not realistic. I think the problem with this helmet is that it’s too perfect, so it looks plastic-y. It doesn’t look handmade or forged in metal like it should be. The concept is cool, but the way it was made makes it lose its realistic value. I also found Calypso’s net costume very distracting. It was overly designed and too much of a fashion choice. All in all, the costumes were better than in Troy for example, but I could go on with other character examples.

posts.caption: Can a mere mortal get in Greek god shape by following Matt Damon's workout plan? With the help of physical therapist and performance coach Dr. Gabe Stump, whom Damon has worked with since 2022, the 55-year-old Academy Award-winning actor had to train hard and eat right to maintain the body of a hero worthy of a generation-spanning epic tale. At the link in bio, one Vogue writer dives deep into the star's blockbuster workout routine—and does a lot of reps—in hopes of achieving a statuesque physique like Odysseus.

posts.caption:
The United States is back in fashion — at least, as far as luxury is concerned. With the US market on the up, brands across the board are expanding their flagship boutiques and venturing further into the country after years of prioritizing slim slices of the coasts. But where is the best place to show up? At the link in bio, @MaddieeSchulz breaks down the vibes, consumer profiles, and luxury hubs to know, beyond New York and LA.
Illustrations: Veronica Dall’Antonia
posts.alt_text: Yesterday, it was announced that France will become the first EU country to ban social media access for under-15s, including platforms like TikTok. This follows recent measures from both the UK and Australia to restrict teen access to addictive platforms. In Australia, data shows that six months into its social media ban, the overall time that young teens spend online hasn’t changed, and the cohort is instead moving to different online spaces unaffected by the restrictions. In the age of social media bans, which platforms might Gen Alpha dominate? @AmyEOBrien investigates at link in bio.

posts.caption: “Here’s a Ref babe in a bathtub to walk you through the business,” a male voice promises in a 30-minute prospectus video, viewed by Vogue Business, in which @Reformation executives, alongside clips of a bubble-bathed model, Champagne flute in-hand, make their case to investors that they should bet on the 2009-founded brand. A nod to the brand’s tagline — “Being naked is the #1 most sustainable option. We’re #2” — the pitch video is designed to give would-be investors a sense not just of Reformation’s financials and growth potential, but of what the brand is all about. US editor @madeleineshculz unpacks the tongue-in-cheek investor pitch at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Images can now be generated instantly, but meaning still has to be accumulated, writes @AmyEObrien. In the last 12 months, luxury brands from @Loewe to @TiffanyAndCo have been investing heavily in showcasing their archives, doubling down on long-term storytelling and platforming craftsmanship — not because they’ve run out of ideas, but because these are the last brand assets AI can’t accelerate. “The past is the last moat,” says @8ball_Sean, co-founder of branding agency @8ghtb4ll. “Everything else is subject to imitation.” Find out how luxury brands can create meaning in the age of AI, at the link in bio.

These designs are being worn at high-profile events globally, including by K-pop groups such as @KatseyeWorld, @BlackpinkOfficial and @Aespa_Official. Contributor @Katicy explores the rise of Vietnamese fashion, at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Navika Kuchakulla and Ashwath Muruganand first met at a Yemeni coffee shop after he spilled tea on her. Muruganand describes himself as reliably clumsy, and Kuchakulla found it endearing. Their wedding in Mexico placed South Indian rituals inside that landscape, with Tamil and Telugu ceremonies, a poolside haldi, a sangeet overlooking the Parroquia and a reception framed by emerald tablescapes and floor-to-ceiling drapery. Kuchakulla moved between a Tarun Tahiliani lehenga and a Kanchi pattu sari custom-woven by Sri Karpaga Vinayagar Silks Kanchipuram. The lehenga was chosen for the outdoor garden setting, while the sari brought in handwoven silk, zari and the cultural weight carried by Kanchipuram saris. To see their wedding up-close, head to the link in bio.
Image credits: @yuko.weddings
#VogueWeddings

posts.caption:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.
She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”
Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.
For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile.
Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.
posts.alt_text:

As she celebrates her 34th birthday today, take a look back at some of the star’s best beauty moments at the link in bio. 
Photographed by @NadineIjewere, Vogue, April 2021

posts.caption: When Spain's national football team brought home the World Cup trophy, they were greeted by a crowd of almost two million people, the prime minister, and the Spanish royal family. For King Felipe VI’s daughters, princesses Leonor and Sofia, it was a full-circle moment, harking back to Spain’s last World Cup win in 2010, when they held the trophy as children. At the link in our bio, discover everything you need to know about the country's royal family, from King Felipe and Queen Letizia to their daughters, and beyond.

With the healthy habits of Sardinians, parts of the Italian island are officially designated as a "Blue Zone"—a region defined for having more centenarians and fewer instances of chronic disease than anywhere else in the world. At the link in our bio, writer @Jessica_Burrell discovers the routines that lead to longevity after completing a four-day stay at Romazzino, a Belmond Hotel set on Sardinia's Costa Smeralda. 
Photographed by Stan Malinowski, Vogue, July 1977

posts.caption:
@Kareem Rahma and Karina Muslimova (@karinziii) tied the knot on June 27 in New York City. When their initial plans for a destination wedding in Cairo, Egypt fell through, the "SubwayTakes" host and the writer pivoted to a last-minute celebration in their home city. “It had to happen," says Kareem. At first, after the couple welcomed their daughter, Sufi, in 2024, they weren't quite sure if marriage was in their future. However, after attending a few of their close friend's weddings, they decided it was something they wanted. "There’s something powerful about bringing all the people you love together to celebrate not just us as a couple, but friendship, family, and life itself," shares the bride. Tap the link in our bio for a look inside their NYC celebration.
Photos: Amir Hamja @amirbangs / @WestThirdFilms

posts.caption: Meet rising catwalk star @JosephenAkuei. The 22-year-old model—who is of South Sudanese heritage—is currently signed with Priscillas Models in Australia and Heroes Model management in New York (she now splits her time between Paris, London, and New York). If the striking model looks familiar, that could be because Akuei has been walking all of the top runways lately: She first made her debut walking Chanel’s spring 2026 show in Paris (Matthieu Blazy’s first!), and has since made cameos at buzzy shows such as Hermès, Givenchy, and Pucci, among others. At the link in our bio, the rising model shares what’s inside her closet with Vogue. (Hint: She perfects the art of high-low dressing.
posts.alt_text: There are few modern stars who capture audiences attention so thoroughly as Zendaya. For her fashion-minded, eagle-eyed fans, every press tour seems to up the anticipation, and though Law Roach and Zendaya approach each film with the same thematic focus, "The Odyssey’s" epic scale inspires its press tour fashion to match. “It’s almost like [everybody is] waiting for the press tour to happen just as much as people are waiting for the movie to come out,” says the actor's longtime stylist. Learn more about the duo's red carpet approach at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Call it the Chanel effect—more women are getting into the historically boys-club category. The classic button-up is a nondescript garment, so you’d be forgiven for not noticing, but women’s shirting has undergone a seismic shift. It's everywhere on the runways and, more importantly, on the rack. More brands are taking on the category from a woman’s point of view, applying beloved elements of classic men’s shirting—construction, quality, and, often for women, an oversized fit—to a woman’s body. Read more about the shift at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Summer 2026 is officially the season of the endless heat wave. This week, France’s scorching La Canicule seemed to hop on a transatlantic flight before parking itself over America’s East Coast, packing mind-melting temps and sweat-inducing humidity in its carry-on. On Wednesday, the heat index in NYC hit 104° F, we quickly learned that New York—always unpredictable, always elusive—failed to conform to any strict uptown/downtown sartorial codes. And that those braving the elements often prioritized looking good over comfort. See more street style from the hottest day of the year (so far) and learn more about these New Yorkers at the link in our bio. Photographed by @MichaellaJelin
posts.alt_text: There are few modern stars who capture audiences attention so thoroughly as Zendaya. For her fashion-minded, eagle-eyed fans, every press tour seems to up the anticipation, and though Law Roach and Zendaya approach each film with the same thematic focus, "The Odyssey’s" epic scale inspires its press tour fashion to match. “It’s almost like [everybody is] waiting for the press tour to happen just as much as people are waiting for the movie to come out,” says the actor's longtime stylist. Learn more about the duo's red carpet approach at the link in bio.

posts.caption: For those who aren’t inclined to wear a jersey for the 2026 World Cup Final, consider the past runways for all your team spirit needs. Discover inspiration straight from the runways at the link in bio.

posts.caption: If you're looking to add more color to your wardrobe, you're in luck. The best part about building on a foundation of black pieces is that introducing color can be as simple as swapping one piece at a time. Black plus a color play well together, whereas going in all at once might have me looking like a popsicle. The goal is integration, not transformation. And the smartest way to do it is gradually, starting small and working your way up, especially for those who find color intimidating. The 2026 pre-fall collections offer a great blueprint. Discover and shop four ways to bring color into your closet at the link in our bio.
